revision.comment: Bot: sync with enwiki
revision.text: {{Documentation subpage}} {{Language templates}} This template marks strings of text as being [[Proto-Indo-European language]] (PIE) content, a [[reconstructed language]] theorised to be the origin of the [[Indo-European languages]] family. PIE text features superscript letters and a number of accents. Reconstructed words feature an asterisk before them as a conventional mark. Reconstructed sentences, phrases and paragraphs do not feature an asterisk at the start. This template '''{{em|does not}}''' automatically italicise text; italics markup must be added '''{{em|around}}''' the template. As PIE is not English, per [[MOS:FOREIGNITALIC]], PIE text {{em|must}} be italicised. ==Examples== The PIE template can be used for paragraphs and singular words. For example: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' Results in: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' An example sentence in this template is: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' Which results in: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' This template {{em|may cause problems}} on longer strings of text; this is because it forces the contents of the template {{em|not}} to wrap, and will instead produce a single long string that extends to the right of the page. '''To use this template for more than a few words,''' add {{para|wrap|normal}} to allow the template to wrap text like a normal sentence or paragraph. == Post-PIE proto-languages == A number of proto-languages followed PIE, which are '''{{em|not reconstructed}}'''. This means that {{tl|lang}} can be used. A number of [[ISO 639-2]] language codes for post-PIE proto-languages are: {{div col}} * ine – [[Indo-European]] * bat – [[Baltic language]] * cu  – [[Old Church Slavonic]] * sla – [[Slavic language]] * orv – [[Old East Slavic]] * non – [[Old Norse]] * ang – [[Old English]] * got – [[Gothic language]] * xvn – [[Vandalic language]] * goh – [[Old High German]] * frk – [[Old Frankish]] * gem – [[Germanic languages]] * sa  – [[Sanskrit]] * inc – [[Indo-Aryan languages]] * peo – [[Old Persian]] * xme – [[Median language]] * xbc – [[Bactrian language]] * xsc – [[Scythian languages|Scythian]] ** xln – Alanian ** oos – Old Ossetian * ira – [[Iranian languages]] * xum – [[Umbrian language]] * xve – [[Venetic]] * xfa – [[Faliscan language]] * fro – [[Old French]] * roa – [[Romance languages]] * xlp – [[Lepontic language]] * xpi – [[Pictish language]] * xce – [[Celtiberian language]] * xcg – [[Cisalpine Gaulish]] * xtg – [[Gaulish|Transalpine Gaulish]] * xga – [[Gaulish|Galatian]] * sga – [[Old Irish]] * cel – [[Celtic languages]] * xcl – [[Classical Armenian]] * hit – [[Hittite language]] ** oht – Old Hittite ** htx – Middle Hittite ** nei – New Hittite * xlu – [[Cuneiform Luwian]] * hlu – [[Hieroglyphic Luwian]] * xlc – [[Lycian language]] * xld – [[Lydian language]] * grc – [[Ancient Greek]] * gmy – [[Mycenaean Greek]] * xmk – [[Ancient Macedonian language]] * xpg – [[Phrygian language]] * xdc – [[Dacian language]] * xto – [[Tocharian languages|Tocharian A]] * txb – [[Tocharian languages|Tocharian B]] {{div col end}} A wider list of [[ISO 639-3]] language codes beginning with 'x', many of which are ancient and extinct languages, can be found [[ISO_639:x|here]]. ==Template data== {{template data header}} { "params": { "1": {}, "wrap": {} }, "paramOrder": [ "1", "wrap" ] } ==See also== *{{tl|IAST}} *{{tl|transliteration}} {{Sandbox other|| [[Category:Indo-European multilingual support templates|{{PAGENAME}}]] [[Category:Lang-x templates]] }}

revision.text: {{Documentation subpage}} {{Language templates}} This template marks strings of text as being [[Proto-Indo-European language]] (PIE) content, a [[reconstructed language]] theorised to be the origin of the [[Indo-European languages]] family. PIE text features superscript letters and a number of accents. Reconstructed words feature an asterisk before them as a conventional mark. Reconstructed sentences, phrases and paragraphs do not feature an asterisk at the start. This template '''{{em|does not}}''' automatically italicise text; italics markup must be added '''{{em|around}}''' the template. As PIE is not English, per [[MOS:FOREIGNITALIC]], PIE text {{em|must}} be italicised. ==Examples== The PIE template can be used for paragraphs and singular words. For example: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' Results in: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' An example sentence in this template is: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' Which results in: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' This template {{em|may cause problems}} on longer strings of text; this is because it forces the contents of the template {{em|not}} to wrap, and will instead produce a single long string that extends to the right of the page. '''To use this template for more than a few words,''' add {{para|wrap|normal}} to allow the template to wrap text like a normal sentence or paragraph. == Post-PIE proto-languages == A number of proto-languages followed PIE, which are '''{{em|not reconstructed}}'''. This means that {{tl|lang}} can be used. A number of [[ISO 639-2]] language codes for post-PIE proto-languages are: {{div col}} * ine – [[Indo-European]] * bat – [[Baltic language]] * cu  – [[Old Church Slavonic]] * sla – [[Slavic language]] * orv – [[Old East Slavic]] * non – [[Old Norse]] * ang – [[Old English]] * got – [[Gothic language]] * xvn – [[Vandalic language]] * goh – [[Old High German]] * frk – [[Old Frankish]] * gem – [[Germanic languages]] * sa  – [[Sanskrit]] * inc – [[Indo-Aryan languages]] * peo – [[Old Persian]] * xme – [[Median language]] * xbc – [[Bactrian language]] * xsc – [[Scythian languages|Scythian]] ** xln – Alanian ** oos – Old Ossetian * ira – [[Iranian languages]] * xum – [[Umbrian language]] * xve – [[Venetic]] * xfa – [[Faliscan language]] * fro – [[Old French]] * roa – [[Romance languages]] * xlp – [[Lepontic language]] * xpi – [[Pictish language]] * xce – [[Celtiberian language]] * xcg – [[Cisalpine Gaulish]] * xtg – [[Gaulish|Transalpine Gaulish]] * xga – [[Gaulish|Galatian]] * sga – [[Old Irish]] * cel – [[Celtic languages]] * xcl – [[Classical Armenian]] * hit – [[Hittite language]] ** oht – Old Hittite ** htx – Middle Hittite ** nei – New Hittite * xlu – [[Cuneiform Luwian]] * hlu – [[Hieroglyphic Luwian]] * xlc – [[Lycian language]] * xld – [[Lydian language]] * grc – [[:en:Ancient Greek|Ancient Greek]] * gmy – [[Mycenaean Greek]] * xmk – [[Ancient Macedonian language]] * xpg – [[Phrygian language]] * xdc – [[Dacian language]] * xto – [[Tocharian languages|Tocharian A]] * txb – [[Tocharian languages|Tocharian B]] {{div col end}} A wider list of [[ISO 639-3]] language codes beginning with 'x', many of which are ancient and extinct languages, can be found [[ISO_639:x|here]]. ==Template data== {{template data header}} { "params": { "1": {}, "wrap": {} }, "paramOrder": [ "1", "wrap" ] } ==See also== *{{tl|IAST}} *{{tl|transliteration}} {{Sandbox other|| [[Category:Indo-European multilingual support templates|{{PAGENAME}}]] [[Category:Lang-x templates]] }}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local/ILL link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text: {{Documentation subpage}} {{Language templates}} This template marks strings of text as being [[Proto-Indo-European language]] (PIE) content, a [[reconstructed language]] theorised to be the origin of the [[Indo-European languages]] family. PIE text features superscript letters and a number of accents. Reconstructed words feature an asterisk before them as a conventional mark. Reconstructed sentences, phrases and paragraphs do not feature an asterisk at the start. This template '''{{em|does not}}''' automatically italicise text; italics markup must be added '''{{em|around}}''' the template. As PIE is not English, per [[MOS:FOREIGNITALIC]], PIE text {{em|must}} be italicised. ==Examples== The PIE template can be used for paragraphs and singular words. For example: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' Results in: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' An example sentence in this template is: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' Which results in: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' This template {{em|may cause problems}} on longer strings of text; this is because it forces the contents of the template {{em|not}} to wrap, and will instead produce a single long string that extends to the right of the page. '''To use this template for more than a few words,''' add {{para|wrap|normal}} to allow the template to wrap text like a normal sentence or paragraph. == Post-PIE proto-languages == A number of proto-languages followed PIE, which are '''{{em|not reconstructed}}'''. This means that {{tl|lang}} can be used. A number of [[:en:ISO 639-2|ISO 639-2]] language codes for post-PIE proto-languages are: {{div col}} * ine – [[Indo-European]] * bat – [[Baltic language]] * cu  – [[Old Church Slavonic]] * sla – [[Slavic language]] * orv – [[Old East Slavic]] * non – [[Old Norse]] * ang – [[Old English]] * got – [[:en:Gothic language|Gothic language]] * xvn – [[Vandalic language]] * goh – [[Old High German]] * frk – [[Old Frankish]] * gem – [[:en:Germanic languages|Germanic languages]] * sa  – [[Sanskrit]] * inc – [[:en:Indo-Aryan languages|Indo-Aryan languages]] * peo – [[Old Persian]] * xme – [[Median language]] * xbc – [[Bactrian language]] * xsc – [[Scythian languages|Scythian]] ** xln – Alanian ** oos – Old Ossetian * ira – [[:en:Iranian languages|Iranian languages]] * xum – [[Umbrian language]] * xve – [[Venetic]] * xfa – [[Faliscan language]] * fro – [[Old French]] * roa – [[Romance languages]] * xlp – [[Lepontic language]] * xpi – [[Pictish language]] * xce – [[Celtiberian language]] * xcg – [[Cisalpine Gaulish]] * xtg – [[Gaulish|Transalpine Gaulish]] * xga – [[Gaulish|Galatian]] * sga – [[Old Irish]] * cel – [[Celtic languages]] * xcl – [[Classical Armenian]] * hit – [[Hittite language]] ** oht – Old Hittite ** htx – Middle Hittite ** nei – New Hittite * xlu – [[Cuneiform Luwian]] * hlu – [[Hieroglyphic Luwian]] * xlc – [[Lycian language]] * xld – [[Lydian language]] * grc – [[:en:Ancient Greek|Ancient Greek]] * gmy – [[Mycenaean Greek]] * xmk – [[Ancient Macedonian language]] * xpg – [[Phrygian language]] * xdc – [[Dacian language]] * xto – [[Tocharian languages|Tocharian A]] * txb – [[Tocharian languages|Tocharian B]] {{div col end}} A wider list of [[:en:ISO 639-3|ISO 639-3]] language codes beginning with 'x', many of which are ancient and extinct languages, can be found [[ISO_639:x|here]]. ==Template data== {{template data header}} { "params": { "1": {}, "wrap": {} }, "paramOrder": [ "1", "wrap" ] } ==See also== *{{tl|IAST}} *{{tl|transliteration}} {{Sandbox other|| [[Category:Indo-European multilingual support templates|{{PAGENAME}}]] [[Category:Lang-x templates]] }}

language]] * xpi – [[Pictish language]] * xce – [[:en:Celtiberian language|Celtiberian language]] * xcg – [[Cisalpine Gaulish]] * xtg – [[Gaulish|Transalpine Gaulish]] * xga – [[Gaulish|Galatian]] * sga – [[Old Irish]] * cel – [[:en:Celtic languages|Celtic languages]] * xcl – [[Classical Armenian]] * hit – [[Hittite language]] ** oht – Old Hittite ** htx – Middle Hittite ** nei – New Hittite * xlu – [[Cuneiform Luwian]] * hlu – [[Hieroglyphic Luwian]] * xlc – [[Lycian language]] * xld – [[Lydian language]] * grc – [[:en:Ancient Greek|Ancient Greek]] * gmy – [[Mycenaean Greek]] * xmk – [[:en:Ancient Macedonian language|Ancient Macedonian language]] * xpg – [[Phrygian language]] * xdc – [[Dacian language]] * xto – [[Tocharian languages|Tocharian A]] * txb – [[Tocharian revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text: {{Documentation subpage}} {{Language templates}} This template marks strings of text as being [[Proto-Indo-European language]] (PIE) content, a [[reconstructed language]] theorised to be the origin of the [[Indo-European languages]] family. PIE text features superscript letters and a number of accents. Reconstructed words feature an asterisk before them as a conventional mark. Reconstructed sentences, phrases and paragraphs do not feature an asterisk at the start. This template '''{{em|does not}}''' automatically italicise text; italics markup must be added '''{{em|around}}''' the template. As PIE is not English, per [[:en:MOS:FOREIGNITALIC|MOS:FOREIGNITALIC]], PIE text {{em|must}} be italicised. ==Examples== The PIE template can be used for paragraphs and singular words. For example: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' Results in: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' An example sentence in this template is: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' Which results in: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' This template {{em|may cause problems}} on longer strings of text; this is because it forces the contents of the template {{em|not}} to wrap, and will instead produce a single long string that extends to the right of the page. '''To use this template for more than a few words,''' add {{para|wrap|normal}} to allow the template to wrap text like a normal sentence or paragraph. == Post-PIE proto-languages == A number of proto-languages followed PIE, which are '''{{em|not reconstructed}}'''. This means that {{tl|lang}} can be used. A number of [[:en:ISO 639-2|ISO 639-2]] language codes for post-PIE proto-languages are: {{div col}} * ine – [[Indo-European]] * bat – [[:en:Baltic language|Baltic language]] * cu  – [[Old Church Slavonic]] * sla – [[Slavic language]] * orv – [[Old East Slavic]] * non – [[:en:Old Norse|Old Norse]] * ang – [[Old English]] * got – [[:en:Gothic language|Gothic language]] * xvn – [[Vandalic language]] * goh – [[Old High German]] * frk – [[Old Frankish]] * gem – [[:en:Germanic languages|Germanic languages]] * sa  – [[Sanskrit]] * inc – [[:en:Indo-Aryan languages|Indo-Aryan languages]] * peo – [[Old Persian]] * xme – [[Median language]] * xbc – [[:en:Bactrian language|Bactrian language]] * xsc – [[Scythian languages|Scythian]] ** xln – Alanian ** oos – Old Ossetian * ira – [[:en:Iranian languages|Iranian languages]] * xum – [[Umbrian language]] * xve – [[Venetic]] * xfa – [[Faliscan language]] * fro – [[Old French]] * roa – [[Romance languages]] * xlp – [[Lepontic language]] * xpi – [[Pictish language]] * xce – [[:en:Celtiberian language|Celtiberian language]] * xcg – [[Cisalpine Gaulish]] * xtg – [[Gaulish|Transalpine Gaulish]] * xga – [[Gaulish|Galatian]] * sga – [[Old Irish]] * cel – [[:en:Celtic languages|Celtic languages]] * xcl – [[Classical Armenian]] * hit – [[Hittite language]] ** oht – Old Hittite ** htx – Middle Hittite ** nei – New Hittite * xlu – [[Cuneiform Luwian]] * hlu – [[Hieroglyphic Luwian]] * xlc – [[Lycian language]] * xld – [[Lydian language]] * grc – [[:en:Ancient Greek|Ancient Greek]] * gmy – [[Mycenaean Greek]] * xmk – [[:en:Ancient Macedonian language|Ancient Macedonian language]] * xpg – [[Phrygian language]] * xdc – [[Dacian language]] * xto – [[Tocharian languages|Tocharian A]] * txb – [[Tocharian languages|Tocharian B]] {{div col end}} A wider list of [[:en:ISO 639-3|ISO 639-3]] language codes beginning with 'x', many of which are ancient and extinct languages, can be found [[ISO_639:x|here]]. ==Template data== {{template data header}} { "params": { "1": {}, "wrap": {} }, "paramOrder": [ "1", "wrap" ] } ==See also== *{{tl|IAST}} *{{tl|transliteration}} {{Sandbox other|| [[Category:Indo-European multilingual support templates|{{PAGENAME}}]] [[Category:Lang-x templates]] }}

revision.text: {{Documentation subpage}} {{Language templates}} This template marks strings of text as being [[:en:Proto-Indo-European language|Proto-Indo-European language]] (PIE) content, a [[reconstructed language]] theorised to be the origin of the [[Indo-European languages]] family. PIE text features superscript letters and a number of accents. Reconstructed words feature an asterisk before them as a conventional mark. Reconstructed sentences, phrases and paragraphs do not feature an asterisk at the start. This template '''{{em|does not}}''' automatically italicise text; italics markup must be added '''{{em|around}}''' the template. As PIE is not English, per [[:en:MOS:FOREIGNITALIC|MOS:FOREIGNITALIC]], PIE text {{em|must}} be italicised. ==Examples== The PIE template can be used for paragraphs and singular words. For example: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' Results in: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' An example sentence in this template is: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' Which results in: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' This template {{em|may cause problems}} on longer strings of text; this is because it forces the contents of the template {{em|not}} to wrap, and will instead produce a single long string that extends to the right of the page. '''To use this template for more than a few words,''' add {{para|wrap|normal}} to allow the template to wrap text like a normal sentence or paragraph. == Post-PIE proto-languages == A number of proto-languages followed PIE, which are '''{{em|not reconstructed}}'''. This means that {{tl|lang}} can be used. A number of [[:en:ISO 639-2|ISO 639-2]] language codes for post-PIE proto-languages are: {{div col}} * ine – [[Indo-European]] * bat – [[:en:Baltic language|Baltic language]] * cu  – [[Old Church Slavonic]] * sla – [[Slavic language]] * orv – [[Old East Slavic]] * non – [[:en:Old Norse|Old Norse]] * ang – [[:en:Old English|Old English]] * got – [[:en:Gothic language|Gothic language]] * xvn – [[Vandalic language]] * goh – [[:en:Old High German|Old High German]] * frk – [[Old Frankish]] * gem – [[:en:Germanic languages|Germanic languages]] * sa  – [[Sanskrit]] * inc – [[:en:Indo-Aryan languages|Indo-Aryan languages]] * peo – [[Old Persian]] * xme – [[Median language]] * xbc – [[:en:Bactrian language|Bactrian language]] * xsc – [[Scythian languages|Scythian]] ** xln – Alanian ** oos – Old Ossetian * ira – [[:en:Iranian languages|Iranian languages]] * xum – [[Umbrian language]] * xve – [[Venetic]] * xfa – [[Faliscan language]] * fro – [[:en:Old French|Old French]] * roa – [[:en:Romance languages|Romance languages]] * xlp – [[Lepontic language]] * xpi – [[Pictish language]] * xce – [[:en:Celtiberian language|Celtiberian language]] * xcg – [[Cisalpine Gaulish]] * xtg – [[Gaulish|Transalpine Gaulish]] * xga – [[Gaulish|Galatian]] * sga – [[Old Irish]] * cel – [[:en:Celtic languages|Celtic languages]] * xcl – [[Classical Armenian]] * hit – [[Hittite language]] ** oht – Old Hittite ** htx – Middle Hittite ** nei – New Hittite * xlu – [[Cuneiform Luwian]] * hlu – [[Hieroglyphic Luwian]] * xlc – [[Lycian language]] * xld – [[Lydian language]] * grc – [[:en:Ancient Greek|Ancient Greek]] * gmy – [[:en:Mycenaean Greek|Mycenaean Greek]] * xmk – [[:en:Ancient Macedonian language|Ancient Macedonian language]] * xpg – [[Phrygian language]] * xdc – [[Dacian language]] * xto – [[Tocharian languages|Tocharian A]] * txb – [[Tocharian languages|Tocharian B]] {{div col end}} A wider list of [[:en:ISO 639-3|ISO 639-3]] language codes beginning with 'x', many of which are ancient and extinct languages, can be found [[ISO_639:x|here]]. ==Template data== {{template data header}} { "params": { "1": {}, "wrap": {} }, "paramOrder": [ "1", "wrap" ] } ==See also== *{{tl|IAST}} *{{tl|transliteration}} {{Sandbox other|| [[Category:Indo-European multilingual support templates|{{PAGENAME}}]] [[Category:Lang-x templates]] }}

This template '''{{em|does not}}''' automatically italicise text; italics markup must be added '''{{em|around}}''' the template. As PIE is not English, per [[:en:MOS:FOREIGNITALIC|MOS:FOREIGNITALIC]], PIE text {{em|must}} be italicised. ==Examples== The PIE template can be used for paragraphs and singular words. For example: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' Results in: ''{{PIE|*wódr̥}}'' An example sentence in this template is: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' Which results in: ''{{PIE|rēḱs deiwos-kʷe}}'' This template {{em|may cause problems}} on longer strings of text; this is because it forces the contents of the template {{em|not}} to wrap, and will instead produce a single long string that extends to the right of the page. '''To use this template for more than a few words,''' add {{para|wrap|normal}} to allow the template to wrap text like a normal sentence or paragraph. == Post-PIE proto-languages == A number of proto-languages followed PIE, which are '''{{em|not reconstructed}}'''. This means that {{tl|lang}} can be used. A number of [[:en:ISO 639-2|ISO 639-2]] language codes for post-PIE proto-languages are: {{div col}} * ine – [[Indo-European]] * bat – [[:en:Baltic language|Baltic language]] * cu  – [[Old Church Slavonic]] * sla – [[Slavic language]] * orv – [[Old East Slavic]] * non – [[:en:Old Norse|Old Norse]] * ang – [[:en:Old English|Old English]] * got – [[:en:Gothic language|Gothic language]] * xvn – [[Vandalic language]] * goh – [[:en:Old High German|Old High German]] * frk – [[Old Frankish]] * gem – [[:en:Germanic languages|Germanic languages]] * sa  – [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]] * inc – [[:en:Indo-Aryan languages|Indo-Aryan languages]] * peo – [[Old Persian]] * xme – [[Median language]] * xbc – [[:en:Bactrian language|Bactrian language]] * xsc – [[Scythian languages|Scythian]] ** xln – Alanian ** oos – Old Ossetian * ira – [[:en:Iranian languages|Iranian languages]] * xum – [[Umbrian language]] * xve – [[Venetic]] * xfa – [[Faliscan language]] * fro – [[:en:Old French|Old French]] * roa – [[:en:Romance languages|Romance languages]] * xlp – [[Lepontic language]] * xpi – [[Pictish language]] * xce – [[:en:Celtiberian language|Celtiberian language]] * xcg – [[Cisalpine Gaulish]] * xtg – [[Gaulish|Transalpine Gaulish]] * xga – [[Gaulish|Galatian]] * sga – [[Old Irish]] * cel – [[:en:Celtic languages|Celtic languages]] * xcl – [[Classical Armenian]] * hit – [[Hittite language]] ** oht – Old Hittite ** htx – Middle Hittite ** nei – New Hittite * xlu – [[Cuneiform Luwian]] * hlu – [[Hieroglyphic Luwian]] * xlc – [[Lycian language]] * xld – [[Lydian language]] * grc – [[:en:Ancient Greek|Ancient Greek]] * gmy – [[:en:Mycenaean Greek|Mycenaean Greek]] * xmk – [[:en:Ancient Macedonian language|Ancient Macedonian language]] * xpg – [[Phrygian language]] * xdc –
